coredata.dc:title: Designed synthesis of chlorine and nitrogen co-doped Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots and their outstanding hydroxyl radical scavenging properties
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Lin
Wang, Zhao
Li, Yan
Wang, Sen
Wang, Lifeng
Qi, Zhaojun
Ge, Qiang
Liu, Xiaoguang
Zhang, Jin Zhong
coredata.dc:description: As a novel zero-dimensional (0D) material, metal carbides and/or carbonitrides (MXenes) quantum dots (MQDs) show unique photoluminescence properties and excellent biocompatibility. However, due to the limited synthesis methods and research to date, many new features have yet to be uncovered. Here, to explore their new properties and expand biological applications, chlorine and nitrogen co-doped Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots (Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs) were designed and synthesized, and their hydroxyl radical scavenging properties were investigated for the first time, revealing outstanding performance. Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs was directly stripped from bulk Ti3AlC2 by electrochemical etching, while N and Cl are successfully introduced to carbon skeleton and Ti boundaries in the etching process by electrochemical reactions between selected electrolytes and Ti3C2 skeleton, respectively. The obtained Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs exhibit large surface-to-volume ratio due to small particle size (ca.3.45 nm) and excellent higher scavenging activity (93.3 %) and lower usage (12.5 μg/mL) towards hydroxyl radicals than the previous reported graphene-based nanoparticles. The underlying mechanism of scavenging activity was also studied based on the reduction experiment with potassium permanganate (KMnO4). The reducing ability of the intrinsic Ti3C2 structure and electron donation of double dopants are the main contributors to the outstanding scavenging activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ti3C2MXene quantum dots
Chlorine and nitrogen co-doping
Hydroxyl radical
Scavenger

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of Cl, N- Ti3C2 MQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs.

SEM images of the surface of bulk Ti3AlC2 before (a) and after (b) electrochemical reaction. (c) TEM image. (d) HR-TEM image. (e) Raman spectrum. (f) XRD pattern of the prepared Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs and Ti3AlC2 powder.
(a) XPS analysis of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) N1s, and (e) Cl2p for Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs.
(a) The photo of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs solution before and after 15 d in the air. (b) The TEM of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs solution after 15 d in the air. (c) The XPS of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs after 15 d in the air.

(a) Absorption spectrum. (b) Survival ratios of RhB with and without Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs in solution after 1 h of UV illumination, absorption spectra of RhB without Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs before illumination was used as control. (c) Photos of RhB dyes solution for fading degree before and after photodegradation reaction. (d) PL emission spectrum of the experimental solution for different volume Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs under light irradiation for 1 h. (e) Absorption spectra of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs and KMnO4 reacted in the dark for 30 min, absorption spectra of KMnO4 without Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs was used as control. (f) Photos of KMnO4 reduction assay before and after photodegradation reaction.
(a) XPS spectra of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs after reaction with KMnO4 and high-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) N1s, (e) Cl2p after the reaction.

Schematic diagram of the scavenging mechanism towards
OH of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs.

Comparison of different kinds of
OH scavengers with the highest scavenging efficiency.

head.ce:title: Designed synthesis of chlorine and nitrogen co-doped Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots and their outstanding hydroxyl radical scavenging properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing, 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of California, Santa Cruz, California 95064, United States
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Biochemistry, University of California, Santa Cruz, California 95064, United States

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors contributed equally to the paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fig. 1 The designed synthesis of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs and its scavenging ability and mechanism towards hydroxyl radicals.

As a novel zero-dimensional (0D) material, metal carbides and/or carbonitrides (MXenes) quantum dots (MQDs) show unique photoluminescence properties and excellent biocompatibility. However, due to the limited synthesis methods and research to date, many new features have yet to be uncovered. Here, to explore their new properties and expand biological applications, chlorine and nitrogen co-doped Ti3C2 MXene quantum dots (Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs) were designed and synthesized, and their hydroxyl radical scavenging properties were investigated for the first time, revealing outstanding performance. Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs was directly stripped from bulk Ti3AlC2 by electrochemical etching, while N and Cl are successfully introduced to carbon skeleton and Ti boundaries in the etching process by electrochemical reactions between selected electrolytes and Ti3C2 skeleton, respectively. The obtained Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs exhibit large surface-to-volume ratio due to small particle size (ca.3.45 nm) and excellent higher scavenging activity (93.3 %) and lower usage (12.5 μg/mL) towards hydroxyl radicals than the previous reported graphene-based nanoparticles. The underlying mechanism of scavenging activity was also studied based on the reduction experiment with potassium permanganate (KMnO4). The reducing ability of the intrinsic Ti3C2 structure and electron donation of double dopants are the main contributors to the outstanding scavenging activity.

ce:section.ce:para:
Hydroxyl radical (
OH) has large electronegativity, strong oxidation electrode potential (2.8 V), and high reactivity with almost all biological molecules [1]. It plays an essential role in photocatalytic degradation of contaminants, antibacteria, soil remediation and optical precision processing [2–5]. However, its high reactivity also brings about side effects, especially in organisms. When in excess, it can cause oxidative damage in the biological structures such as cell membranes, proteins, and DNA [6]. Furthermore, it can cause various acute and chronic diseases and give some side effects to the health of living organism [7]. Therefore, while
OH is of great significance for environmental protection and materials engineering, it is of strong interest to develop hydroxyl radical scavengers for disease prevention and treatment purposes. However, there are few scavengers available because of the lack of safe and effective candidates.

Metal carbides and/or carbonitrides (MXenes) quantum dots (MQDs) have shown biocompatibility and useful optical properties [8,9]. They have wide biomedical applications such as biomarkers, biosensor, biological imaging, and cancer treatment [10–13]. Their photoluminescence properties mainly result from quantum confinement effect due to their small size. The biocompatibility is ascribed to their corresponding precursors, the two-dimensional (2D) MXenes, that have good biocompatibility and electron transfer capacity because of the complete metal atomic layers and abundant of surface hydrophilic groups [14]. Recently, it has been found that 2D MXene is easily oxidized in water or air, and the oxidation reaction starts from edges and defects [15,16]. Furthermore, Niobium carbide 2D MXenes can react with reactive oxygen radicals (ROS) such as
OH, hydrogen peroxide and superoxide radicals, ending up with Nb-based oxides. The reactive mechanism is attributed to the intrinsic reduction characteristics of MXene nanosheets [17]. Based on the structural similarity between 2D MXenes and the derived MQDs, and higher density of edges and defects of the latter than the former, nano-sized MQDs could be expected to serve as effective ROS scavengers. To our best knowledge, however, there has been no reports on their antioxidant activity for ROS scavenging.

In general, MQDs can be obtained by a two-step approach. The first step is to fabricate MXene nanosheets by selectively chemically etching layer A from the MAX phase, because A layer atoms are usually active and weakly bonded [11,13,18,19]. In the second step, the MXene nanosheets are fragmented into small sizes by such methods as hydrothermal shearing, acid/alkaline reflux or ultrasonication [12,20,21]. These preparation processes not only involve with the use of noxious hydrofluoric acid (HF) or fluoride compounds (such as LiF/HCl, NaHF2, KHF2), but also need an environment of high temperature and pressure [10,22,23]. Thus, it is desirable to explore new methods for direct and environmentally friendly preparation of MQDs.

In addition, elemental doping in nanomaterials can efficaciously modulate their properties including electronic, surface and local chemical [24]. Doping of N, Cl, and P has been widely applied in carbon nanomaterials, e,g., graphene quantum dots, carbon dots and graphene, and found to enhance their antioxidant activity because of the improved electron transfer ability [25–27]. Similarly, doping N, S, and Ru in MXene or MQDs can effectively adjust their electrochemical performance and enhance photoelectron transfer ability, thereby improving their luminescence characteristics and electrocatalytic activity [28–31]. Therefore, it is of great significance to synthesize doped MQDs especially co-doped MQDs and investigate their antioxidant activity.

In this work, a facile, energy-saving, and green electrochemical route was used to directly strip bulk Ti3AlC2 MAX phase to Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs. This stripping process not only has the advantage of low energy consumption because of the excellent conductivity of bulk Ti3AlC2, but also provides effective two-element co-doping way by selecting proper electrolytes. The obtained Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs have small size (ca. 3.45 nm), where the N, Cl atoms are introduced to carbon skeleton and attached to the Ti boundaries, respectively. Meanwhile, they have high stability, can be stable for half a month without deterioration. Furthermore, Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs show high scavenging activity towards
OH based on both dye protection and fluorescence tests. The scavenging mechanism was explored by KMnO4 reduction experiment and the related XPS analysis, where the reducing property of intrinsic Ti3C2 structure and electron donation capability from dopants are considered to be the main contributors to the high scavenging efficiency.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Cl, N- Ti3C2 MQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs were prepared by a potential static method in a two-electrode system using a CHI 660D electrochemical workstation. In this experiment, bulk commercial Ti3AlC2 (Kaixi ceramic powder company, 0.5 cm × 0.5 cm × 2 cm) was used as the working electrode, and a Pt wire as the counter electrode. The electrolyte consisted of 0.10 M tetramethylammonium hydroxide (TMAOH) and 0.20 M NH4Cl. The voltage and time were 0.1 V and 1 h, respectively. The prepared electrolyte solution was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 15 min, and the supernatant was taken and re-centrifuged until no precipitate was present in the solution. The resulting solution was first filtered with a 220 nm filter, and then dialyzed in a cellulose ester membrane bag (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) for 3 days to obtain an aqueous solution of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs (0.10 mg/mL).

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) test was performed by the JSM-7401 electron microscope. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) test was conducted using JEM-2010 electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) test was achieved on the DMAX-RB instrument using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was accomplished by ESCALAB 250Xi electron spectrometer, and Al monochromatic Kα radiation (hυ = 1486.6 eV) source was used. Raman spectroscopic test was carried out using a RM 2000 micro-confocal Raman spectrometer (Renishaw PLC, England), and a He-Ne laser with an excitation wavelength of 632.8 nm was utilized. UV–vis spectroscopy was performed using TU-1900 spectrophotometer and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of samples were obtained by F97 pro (Shanghai Lengguang Technology Co., Ltd).

Preparing 2 mL of sample solution, in which PBS, TiO2, Rhodamine B (RhB) and Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs concentrations are 25 mM, 50 μg/mL, 10 μM and 100 μg/mL respectively. Afterwards, the sample solution was irradiated with an 8 W ultraviolet lamp (365 nm) for 1 h. The residual RhB concentration was determined by absorption spectroscopy (552 nm).

50 μL of PBS solution (0.10 M), 20 μL of TiO2 suspension (5.0 mg/mL), 100 μL of terephthalic acid (0.01 M) and different volumes of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs solution (100 μg/mL) were mixed to prepare a 2.0 mL mixed solution. The luminescence intensity of the sample was tested by exciting with the wavelength of 315 nm after 1 h.

KMnO4 reduction experiment was conducted following the protocol by Ruiz [32]. A 2.0 mL (pH = 3) acid solution containing 100 mM KMnO4 and 100 μg/mL Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs was reacted in the dark for 30 min. The residual KMnO4 concentration was monitored using UV–vis absorption spectroscopy (515 nm).

In the preparation of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs, it is important to select an appropriate electrolyte. In this work, TMAOH and NH4Cl aqueous were chosen as the electrolyte for two reasons. On the one hand, Cl− has a strong binding affinity to Al [30]. On the other hand, N-containing electrolyte ions or small molecules can promote N doping in graphene structures driven by electric potential [24]. Fig. 1 illustrates the synthesis scheme for Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs. In this two-electrode system, Cl− with etching effects first preferentially etches surface Al atoms and breaks the Ti-Al bonds. Then NH4OH is intercalated and opens the edges of the etched working electrode by diffusion of the electrolyte ions [33]. Accompanied with the dissociation of Al atoms and the formation of AlCl3 in the solution, Ti and C layers with large area will be exposed to electrolyte environment. Under the driving forces of the electric potential, the carbon layer fragments through the ion-carbon interaction at the defects or active edges. Meanwhile, Cl− interacts with Ti to form Ti-Cl termination. In this way, MQDs are gradually stripped from the bulk Ti3AlC2 working electrodes, while N is effectively intercalated into the carbon structure through new chemical bond formation with surface adsorbed N-containing electrolyte ions [33]. Finally, Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs are obtained.

This electrochemical preparation and doping process was confirmed by examining the working electrode and detailed MQDs characterization. The surface morphology of the working electrode was first observed by SEM. Before etching, the SEM image in Fig. 2 (a) of bulk Ti3AlC2 shows a smooth surface. However, after etching the surface became rougher and furrier (Fig. 2(b)). This indicated that the working electrode was etched by the electrochemical process, and Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs were stripped off from the surface. Next, TEM image of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs in Fig. 2(c) shows their ultra-small size with an average of ca. 3.45 nm (inset in Fig. 2(c)) and excellent monodispersity. The high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 2(d) reveals the crystal lattice of 0.25 nm, corresponding to the (006) facet of the Ti3C2 layer. Furthermore, the Raman spectrum of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs in Fig. 2(e) shows the disappearance of the vibration modes related to Al-Ti from 150 cm−1 to 300 cm−1, confirming the complete removal of the Al layer and the retaining of Ti-C skeleton structure. The XRD pattern (Fig. 2(f)) of Ti3AlC2 working electrode exhibits many characteristic peak of the Ti3AlC2 phase. However, almost none of these characteristic peaks is detected in the N-Ti3C2 MQDs sample. This suggests that the obtained MQDs are mainly amorphous but the Ti-C layer is retained [11].

The composition, element doping, and surface functional groups were further studied by XPS. In Fig. 3 (a), the peaks at 284.0, 460.0, 532.0, 201.0 and 400.0 eV demonstrates that Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs are composed of C, Ti, O and a small amount of Cl (3.87 at. %) and N (3.00 at. %), respectively. The C1s and O1s spectra (Fig. 3(b) and 3(c)) show the presence of C-O, Ti-OH terminations and TiO2. Meanwhile, N1s spectrum (Fig. 3(d)) exhibits graphite N (401.0 eV), pyrrole N (339.1 eV), and N-H bond (400.2 eV) [34]. Furthermore, the Cl2p spectrum, (Fig. 3(e)) indicates Ti-Cl and N-Cl bonding [25,35]. All the above results suggest that Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs are formed in the electrochemical etching process from the electrode surface. They are mostly amorphous but contain the Ti-C skeleton, in which the doped N bonds with C/H or replaces C in the C layer, but Cl bonds with Ti. Some oxygen functional groups, mainly −OH, and partly oxidized TiO2 are also present on the MQDs surface.

Before the investigation of scavenging activity of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs, the stability was first studied. After keeping Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs solution in air for 15 days, the solution remains homogeneous without any aggregation or color change (Fig. 4 (a)). And the TEM image (Fig. 4(b)) and XPS spectra (Fig. 4(c)) of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs also exhibits similar morphology characteristic, component and content as that of before. These results indicate that the stability of the as-prepared Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs has been greatly improved compared with their two dimensional MXenes [36,37].

To investigate the scavenging activity of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs against
OH, RhB dye protection was used to monitor the
OH concentration.
OH was generated by TiO2 solution under UV light, it can attack the conjugated structure of RhB molecules, leading to the degradation and fading of RhB [38]. As shown in Fig. 5 (a)–(c), almost complete fading and the nearly zero absorption intensity of RhB solution were observed after its reaction with
OH for 1 h. However, in the presence of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs, there was little change in color and absorption intensity, suggesting Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs have scavenging capability towards
OH. Furthermore, the high sensitive fluorescence probe approach was introduced to track
OH. In this experiment, trephthalic acid (TA) was used as an indicator because it can capture
OH to form 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid with photoluminescence (PL) signals (excitation wavelength: 315 nm, emission peak: 430 nm). After 1 h reaction, the PL intensity of the sample containing Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs decreases compared to the control (without MQDs) sample. The higher the content of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs, the lower the PL intensity of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (Fig. 5(d)). When Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs reaches to 12.5 μg/mL, almost no fluorescence can be detected, indicating the absence of
OH.

Compared to the well-known CQDs, the as-prepared Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs have higher scavenging activity. As shown in Table 1 , in the CQDs test,
OH is usually produced by two approaches. One is photo-Fenton reaction from H2O2, with
OH produced at high concentration in a short period of time. The undoped CQDs, Ce-CQDs, N/S-CQDs, and Se-CQDs exhibited scavenging ability (≤90 %) [39–43]. However, this scavenging process usually requires a high concentration of CQDs, which is a challenge for CQDs preparation and storage. Another approach to obtain
OH at low concentration is to irradiate TiO2 under UV. This method is favored when long-term scavenging ability and a small amount of scavenger are demanded. As can be seen, Cl-GQDs and GQDs-18 with 50 μg/mL are needed to maintain relatively high activity (91.0 % and 81.7 %). We also used the latter method to measure the scavenging activity of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs as shown above. Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs exhibited high scavenging activity (93.3 %) and lower usage (12.5 μg/mL), demonstrating their excellent scavenging activity against
OH.

In order to explore the scavenging mechanism of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs, the analysis on the changes of the valence state or elemental composition before and after the scavenging reaction would be desirable. However, this is a challenge to do such an experiment, because it involves many materials (such as TiO2, TPA) and is difficult to purify. Therefore, the reduction experiment of KMnO4 was introduced instead and the valence state changes of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs were investigated by XPS afterwards. In Fig. 5(e), the characteristic absorption peaks of KMnO4 from 450 nm to 600 nm disappears after reacting with Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs in dark for 30 min. Correspondingly, its solution changes from purple to pale yellow in Fig. 5(f), indicating that KMnO4 is rapidly reduced by Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs, and the valence state of Mn changes from Mn (VII) to Mn (IV) [44]. Then, the XPS spectra of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs after the reaction were measured. The survey spectrum in Fig. 6 (a) exhibits an obvious improved O1s photoelectron peak besides the C, Ti, Cl and N. Furthermore, the C1s spectrum (Fig. 6(b)) shows a small amount of COC and COOH bonds. Fitting of the O1s spectrum (Fig. 6(c)) shows that O still mainly exists in the form of TiO2 and C/Ti-(OH)x, but TiO2 content increases. N is still about 3.00 at %, but the contents of pyrrole N and graphite N decreases by 10.02 % and 15.46 %, respectively, while the content of N-H increases by 25.49 %. Cl 2p is deconvoluted into Ti-Cl and Cl-O bonds (Fig. 6(e)). The Cl 2p content is reduced and the Ti-Cl binding energy moves to a higher energy direction, which indicates that the electron density of Cl element is reduced.

Combining the above analyses, a model of the scavenging mechanism of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs for
OH is proposed, as illustrated in Fig. 7 . The reducing property of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs is suggested to be responsible for the scavenging activity. Meanwhile, the redox reaction takes place on the surface of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs. Since Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs and
OH are both present in the solution and Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs are very small (3.45 nm) and have large surface area relative to volume, they can have close contact to each other.
OH has strong oxidation electrode potential (2.8 V) and can oxidize Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs starting from the edge and surface defects. Therefore, a large number of -OH groups is formed on the surface of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs. Then, dehydration of adjacent -OH groups become COC, COOH bonds and TiO2 on the surface. This is the main scavenging process of
OH, which can explain the increase of total oxygen content, the appearance COC, COOH bonds and increased TiO2 in XPS spectrum after reaction.

In addition, electron donation from the doped Cl and N elements in MQDs also play important roles on
OH inactivation. It is known that pyrrole N in carbon skeleton has good electron donating characteristics and high charge mobility, so electrons can be transferred from pyrrole N to
OH to inactivate the radicals [45]. The reduction of pyrrole N and increase in N-H content after the reaction in XPS spectra, demonstrating the electron donation ability of pyrrole N contributes to the
OH scavenging. Moreover, compared with C and Ti, Cl has a larger atomic radius and higher electronegativity (Cl 3.1, C 2.5, Ti 1.8), and surface Ti-Cl bonds can promote the electronic interaction between MQDs and free radicals [46]. In the Ti-Cl bond, Cl is highly electronegative, so the ability to attract electrons in the compound is relatively strong. Free radicals have strong oxidizing properties, so when MQDs and free radicals contact, Cl will act as an electron donor to provide electrons, the Ti-Cl bond is broken, and Ti is oxidized at the same time, thereby promoting the electronic interaction between MQDs and free radicals. As shown in XPS spectra, the decrease in Cl content and the reduction in electron density indicate that Cl acts as an electron donor and may provide electrons to
OH. Therefore, the electron donation from doped Cl and N elements accelerate the removal of
OH. In all, the scavenging activity of Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs is mainly originated from the reducing property of Ti3C2 structure, which is oxidized by the
OH, and the electron donation capabilities from the doped N and Cl elements.

In summary, we have synthesized Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs by a facile and efficient electrochemical method. The obtained Cl, N-Ti3C2 MQDs have high surface area and show excellent scavenging activity towards
OH. The co-doping with Cl and N is important in improving the properties of the MQDs by altering their electronic properties. KMnO4 reduction experiment and valence state analysis by XPS helped to reveal the scavenging mechanism. The strong scavenging activity is attributed to the reducing ability of the intrinsic Ti3C2 structure as well as the electron donation from the dopants. This work demonstrates potential applications of MXenes based QDs in radical scavenging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21674011, 21404008), Beijing Municipal Natural Science Foundation (Grant No. 2172040), Beijing Organization department outstanding talented person project (2013D009006000001) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (FRF-GF-17-B11).
